title: Widgets ã Stateless & Stateful
objectives: Understand widgets via a real-life analogy
Know Stateless vs Stateful and when to use each
Write clean widget code with `const`, composition, and small classes
Distinguish widget vs element vs render object

sections.markdown:
**Think of a movie set built with LEGO blueprints.**

- Every LEGO character on set is a `Widget` ã but a widget isn't the *physical* character. It's the **blueprint / instruction sheet** describing what the character looks like RIGHT NOW: 'hat=red, pose=standing, holding=sword'.
- Each frame of the movie, the studio rebuilds a fresh set of blueprints (rebuilds widgets). That sounds wasteful ã but the studio's assistant (Flutter's `Element` tree) compares old vs new blueprint and reuses actual bricks (`RenderObject`s) wherever possible. Only the *changed* bricks get replaced.
- A **`StatelessWidget`** is a blueprint that never changes on its own ã a background pattern brick. Its output depends only on its inputs.
- A **`StatefulWidget`** is a blueprint bundled with a small stateful controller (`State`) ã like a character whose blueprint includes 'facial expression: happy ã sad ã surprised' that can change over time without regenerating the whole character.

**The mental shift:** widgets are cheap, throw-away descriptions. Build them, describe the UI you want, let Flutter figure out the minimal diff. Do NOT try to hold onto references or mutate widgets ã they're immutable by design.

**A Widget** is an immutable description of part of a UI. Flutter's UI is a *tree of widgets*, rebuilt (in whole or part) whenever state changes. **StatelessWidget** ã no internal state; renders purely from its constructor parameters. **StatefulWidget** ã pairs an immutable widget-config with a mutable `State` object that persists across rebuilds and can call `setState()` to trigger a new build.
## StatelessWidget vs StatefulWidget

rows: No ã pure function of props

Anything with UI state (form field, tab, animation)
When parent rebuilds and props change
When parent rebuilds OR `setState()` is called
Very cheap ã a few pointers
Slightly more (State object retained across rebuilds)

## The three trees (mental model)

rows: Wires widgets to render objects; holds `BuildContext`s and `State` objects

sections.markdown: **The insight:** when you 'rebuild' in Flutter, you're rebuilding CHEAP widget descriptions. Flutter reuses the same expensive Element + RenderObject wherever the widget type + key line up. That's why calling `setState` is fast ã Flutter diffs, not repaints from scratch.
## Common beginner mistakes

**Mistake 1: Making everything Stateful.** Turns cheap `const` widgets into stateful ones ã creates a `State` object per widget and blocks `const` optimizations. Rule: **Stateless unless it needs to hold state.**

**Mistake 2: Forgetting `const`.** `const Text('Home')` is skip-rebuilt (Flutter knows it can't have changed). `Text('Home')` builds a new object each frame. Prefix EVERY constructor you can with `const`.

**Mistake 3: Building giant widget trees inside one `build()`.** Slower to rebuild, hard to read. Extract subtrees into their own StatelessWidget ã Flutter can skip rebuilding them when props haven't changed.

**Mistake 4: Mutating state without `setState()`.** `_count++` alone won't rebuild ã Flutter never knows. Always `setState(() => _count++)`. Alternative: use a state-management library (Riverpod/Bloc) so this pattern goes away.

**Mistake 5: Doing work in `build()` that shouldn't be per-build.** `build()` may be called MANY times per second. Don't allocate expensive objects, decode images, or start async work there. Use `initState` for one-shot setup, or move work to a controller.
## Best practices
**1. Default to StatelessWidget.** Only reach for Stateful when you have local UI state (a form field, an expanded flag, an animation controller).
**2. Prefix EVERY constructor with `const` where possible.** The most consistent Flutter perf win ã flat-out skips rebuilds of subtrees.

**3. Extract subtrees into small widgets.** A 300-line `build()` is a code smell. `_UserAvatar`, `_OrderTile`, `_HeaderBar` ã small widgets are readable AND perf-friendly (Flutter can skip rebuilding them).
**4. Pass callbacks, not BuildContext.** Don't hand off `context` between widgets ã it becomes stale. Pass the specific data or callback the child needs.
**5. Use `Key`s intentionally.** Use `ValueKey` on list items that could reorder ã Flutter reuses the right State object. Use `GlobalKey` sparingly (they leak if held past their widget's lifetime).
**6. For non-trivial state, use a state-management solution.** Riverpod, Bloc, Provider ã anything but ad-hoc `setState` for cross-widget state.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) ã Refactor to Stateless.** Take a Stateful widget with no state and make it Stateless. Watch the widget code shrink by half.
**Practice 2 (Easy) ã Add `const` everywhere.** Prefix every widget constructor with `const` where the analyzer accepts it. Measure APK size / hot-reload speed.
**Practice 3 (Medium) ã Counter.** Build the sample; add reset and decrement.
**Practice 4 (Medium) ã Extract subtrees.** Take a 200-line `build()`, extract 5 small widgets, verify hot-reload still works.
**Practice 5 (Hard) ã Expandable card.** Stateful widget with `_expanded` bool; animated with `AnimatedContainer`.
**Practice 6 (Expert) ã Reorderable list with keys.** Make sure state persists on each item across reorder ã that's what `ValueKey(item.id)` is for.
## Interview Q&A ã with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is a widget?**

**A:** A widget is an **immutable description of part of a UI**. It's a Dart class extending `Widget` (usually via `StatelessWidget` or `StatefulWidget`). Widgets are cheap to construct and thrown away every rebuild. The framework diffs new widgets against the existing **Element tree**, reusing Elements and RenderObjects wherever the widget type + key match. So widgets aren't 'the UI' ã they're **blueprints of the UI you want**. Flutter figures out the minimal set of actual changes needed. This lets Flutter's declarative model (`build()` returns a fresh tree) coexist with efficient incremental rendering.

**Q2: StatelessWidget vs StatefulWidget?**

**A:** **StatelessWidget** ã no internal state. Its `build()` is a pure function of the widget's fields. Cheap, easy to reason about. Use for presentational widgets. **StatefulWidget** ã pairs an immutable widget-config with a separate mutable `State` object that survives rebuilds. The `State` holds internal data (a counter, an animation controller, a scroll position). Its `build()` lives on the State. Use when you need to hold local UI state that persists across rebuilds. **Rule:** start Stateless; only promote to Stateful when you have data that must survive rebuilds locally to that widget.

**Q3: Why is a StatefulWidget split into two classes?**

**A:** Because the WIDGET is immutable (part of the widget tree, replaced every rebuild) but its STATE isn't ã the State object needs to survive rebuilds. If they were one class, either the widget couldn't be `final` (breaks tree diffing) or state would reset every rebuild. Splitting solves both: the immutable widget-config replaces itself freely; the State object is retained by the Element tree as long as the widget type + key stay the same. This is why `createState()` returns a fresh State object once, and the framework reuses it as long as it can.

**Q4: Why prefix widgets with `const`?**

**A:** `const` widgets are compile-time constants ã the compiler canonicalizes them (identical values share one instance), and Flutter's diff algorithm **skip-rebuilds** any subtree whose top widget is `const` ã because its value literally cannot have changed. Extract stable subtrees like icons, dividers, headers, empty placeholders as `const` widgets and Flutter avoids rebuilding them entirely. It's one of the cheapest and most consistent Flutter perf wins; every serious codebase enforces it via `prefer_const_constructors` / `prefer_const_literals_to_create_immutables` lints.

**Q5: What is `setState()` and what does it do?**

**A:** `setState(fn)` schedules the current `State` object to rebuild on the NEXT frame. It runs `fn` synchronously (that's where you mutate state), marks the Element dirty, and Flutter will re-invoke `build()` before the next paint. **What it does NOT do:** rebuild the whole app, run immediately, or accept an async function. **Rules:** (1) `fn` should be synchronous; do the async work OUTSIDE and call `setState` with the result. (2) Don't call `setState` after `dispose()` ã check `mounted`. (3) `setState` is expensive to abuse ã every call schedules a rebuild ã so state-management libraries (Riverpod/Bloc) avoid it by using observable state elsewhere.

**Q6: What is a `Key` and when do you use one?**

**A:** A `Key` uniquely identifies a widget across rebuilds so Flutter can match it to the correct Element. Without keys, Flutter matches by **position + widget-type** ã usually fine, but breaks when widgets of the same type reorder (e.g., a list of `TextField`s that reshuffle). Types: **`ValueKey(id)`** ã most common, use on list items; **`ObjectKey(obj)`** ã key by object identity; **`GlobalKey`** ã a key visible across the whole tree, lets you access a widget's State from anywhere (avoid ã use state management instead); **`UniqueKey()`** ã a fresh key every rebuild (forces re-creation of state). **Rule:** add `ValueKey(item.id)` to items in a list that can reorder or filter.

**Q7: Widget vs Element vs RenderObject ã what's the difference?**

**A:** **Widget** ã the blueprint you write in Dart. Cheap, immutable, thrown away and rebuilt often. **Element** ã the framework's runtime instantiation of a widget in the tree. Holds a `BuildContext`, ties the widget to its `RenderObject`, retains `State` between rebuilds. Roughly analogous to a virtual DOM node. **RenderObject** ã the layout + paint + hit-test machinery. Expensive: has a canvas, a size, a paint region, sits in the render tree. When you rebuild widgets, Flutter tries to reuse the same Element (and its RenderObject) wherever possible; only if the widget's type OR key changed does Flutter create a fresh Element + RenderObject. This layered architecture is why Flutter's rebuild is fast even with a large tree.

## Real-world usage

- **Every Flutter screen** ã a tree of widgets, some Stateless, some Stateful.
- **Material / Cupertino / your app widgets** ã all built on the same primitives (`StatelessWidget`, `StatefulWidget`, `RenderObjectWidget`).
- **Custom widgets** ã 90% Stateless, 10% Stateful (mostly for animation controllers or form field state).
- **Design systems** ã a design-system package is a curated set of Stateless widgets.
- **Testing** ã `WidgetTester.pumpWidget(...)` builds the widget tree; `find.byType(Counter)` navigates it.

## Advanced notes

- **`InheritedWidget`** ã the base for propagating data down the tree (Theme, MediaQuery, Provider). Efficient because it only rebuilds descendants that read from it.
- **`InheritedNotifier` / `InheritedModel`** ã advanced variants for finer-grained rebuilding.
- **`ProxyWidget` and `RenderObjectWidget`** ã how framework-level widgets like `Container`, `Padding`, `Align` are built.
- **`Diagnosticable` mixin** ã `debugFillProperties` for nicer widget inspector output.
- **`hashCode`/`==` on widget classes** ã Flutter uses widget type + key for identity; ==/hashCode DON'T need to consider all fields (that would defeat immutability).
- **`StatefulWidget` with `TickerProviderStateMixin`** ã for animation controllers that consume a ticker.
- **`AutomaticKeepAliveClientMixin`** ã keep a widget's State alive across parent-tab switches (e.g., TabBar pages).
- **`didUpdateWidget(oldWidget)`** ã called when the parent rebuilds with a NEW widget-config; use to react to prop changes.

flashcards.back: Immutable description of part of the UI; a blueprint, not the UI itself.
Stateless = pure fn of props. Stateful = pairs immutable widget with a mutable State that survives rebuilds.
Compile-time canonicalized; Flutter skip-rebuilds their subtree ã huge perf win.
Marks the current State dirty; Flutter rebuilds it on the next frame.
Widget (blueprint) ã Element (runtime instance + context) ã RenderObject (layout/paint).
On list items that can reorder or filter ã preserves the right State object.
